sentences.text: No, she never returned.
Neither did her mother ever return.
And I believe that she was killed right there downstairs in this cellar where they were beating them and trying to convince her that she should admit that they're Jewish.
I, I probably was six.
I thought that they killed her.
Because I already was surrounded by the knowledge of killing.
And I knew already that what they're doing is killing.
And I knew that they torture.
And they kill.
And they beat.
And so I, I figured if she's not talking or screaming or coming back, then she's killed.
Because I want to tell you, the, the upstairs of the Grey House was the commando, the, the offices, the headquarters of the Gestapo that ruled over Plaszow.
And the Grey House was kind of their-- the pet playhouse.
They used to take people there that they could do bad things to, worse than what they were doing to the people in the camps.
Or maybe I shouldn't say worse.
Because they did terrible things to everybody.
But a lot of people in that Grey House did not come out alive.
And that's why it had the reputation of the Grey House.
And in Polish they called Szary Domek, the Grey House.
Only the four of us, and when we arrived, this one little girl.
How-- I was very, very scared because I only heard terrible things.
And we couldn't even look outside.
There was not even a window.
The windows were barricaded by wood.
And there was just a little hole in one of the woods in the window.
And we used to take turns who is going to look at the sky or at the light of the day.
So among all the people that were down there, everybody got a chance to walk up to this hole and look.
And I remember my aunt letting us go and saying, I want you to every day look at the light of day.
And through this little hole, I saw the light of day.
And today, when I see the light of day, and I see people walking and running and doing things, and they don't stop to look at the light of day.
They do things.
And, and they don't realize that the whole purpose is to really enjoy the light of day-- that you live.
And you're out.
And you're free.
And they're really missing out a lot.
Nothing, absolutely nothing-- just sit all day long, nothing.
The highlight of the day was when the Nazis came and brought us the bread, which was three times a day, and the soup.
When they came and started screaming and yelling that we need to stand in line, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] half the time, we would cry because we were so scared.
Most of the time, we were just afraid to move because who is looking through the hole?
And maybe we're doing something that will get them angry.
If, if we sit, maybe they'll angry.
If we stand, maybe they'll be angry.
Maybe they'll come and take us and kill us.
So it was all the time afraid.
Outdoors-- they would take us outdoors, quickly count us up, get us back indoors.
And after a while, they would not take us outdoors.
They would just do it indoors downstairs.
And the only highlight of our lives was when [?
Karli ?] came with his German Shepherd.
He let us caress his dog.
He talked to us.
And he said, don't be afraid, and I'll bring you something to eat.
And that was the highlight of our life in the Grey House.
Maybe 30-- but all the time, they were taking out people.
And at the end, at the end, before they took us away from there, maybe for a week, it was just the two aunts and the four children.
I don't know.
It's just like today, whoever meets to me and asks me where were you during the war, and I say I was in the concentration camp, they say were you a little child?
And I say yes.
All the time.
All the time, all the time that, that one little prayer that I knew.
[HEBREW SPEECH] Meaning, you know our God is one.
And our God is our God.
And I had such faith that maybe it's going to be all right.
Yes.
Yes.
From here, they took us to a place called Breslau.
And in Breslau, it was not a-- it wasn't a concentration camp.
It was more like houses, houses built from, from-- you know, like houses are built, not wood but maybe cement.
And there, I saw my uncle again.
And they brought there the men and the women.
And I remember the most heartbreaking goodbye that I have ever seen was the goodbye that my aunt and uncle said to each other in Breslau.
Because I think that they realized that this is for real.
And they're not going to go home, that they're going to be taken somewhere.
And maybe they'll never see each other again.
And what I forgot to tell you about Wieliczka is that my father's brother lived in Wieliczka.
And he had three daughters.
One was my age, which at-- so at the time she was six.
And one was 12.
And one was 17.
And their name was-- the youngest one was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Geisha, Lola, and Bela.
And they were stunning.
They were so beautiful that I think today if they were taken to a contest, they would win a beauty contest.
They were really magnificent-looking girls.
And my uncle, my father's brother, was very much concerned that if the Nazis caught these girls that they might use them sexually.
So he made arrangement with some kind of a peasant from Wieliczka, a Polish peasant, to take them over the border to some other country.
And my uncle gave all his savings that he had and asked this peasant that if he does that, and after the war he will get more money.
So the peasant went.
And he came back three days later.
And he came to my uncle in the house where the other uncle lived, this uncle Max Katz.
And this uncle's name was Yisrael Landau.
And he also called himself Isaac Landau.
And he said to him, I'm really sorry.
But when we got to the border, the Nazis took your girls.
And I don't even want to tell you what happened to them.
And I remember my uncle started screaming and pulling his hair, and just, he almost went crazy.
So that, that were my first cousins, three of my first cousins.
So now we--
Are in Breslau, right.
So now, the aunt and the uncle said goodbye to each other.
And they took all the men away.
And they took everybody's-- whatever personal things that were still left with us, they took now everything away from us.
Like my aunt had little earrings, like little gold dots in her ear.
They took that.
And my little cousin, her daughter, also had these little, you know, gold dots they used to put in little girls.
They took that.
And then my aunt had a little wedding band, a gold wedding band.
They took that.
They took everything from the men.
And they took all the men away.
And then we were in Breslau.
And they told all of us to get undressed.
And the aunt, the mother of the boy-- his name was [INAUDIBLE].
And I'm sure that was like a-- maybe was Josef and something else.
But that's what they called him.
And his mother, his name-- her name was [INAUDIBLE].
And the other aunt was [INAUDIBLE] also.
Both aunts the-- had the same name.
And she was a daughter of a very famous rabbi.
And she said, I'm not getting undressed.
And they started beating her.
And they said, you are.
And she said, no.
And they were beating her and beating her and she was screaming and crying.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and this other aunt that was the mother my cousin, she also was religious.
In fact, she used to wear a, a sheitel.
She wore a wig.
Both women wore wigs.
And they took that away from them, which was a great shock for them because that's how they were brought up, never to uncover their hair in front of men.
And I know it was a Saturday.
And I don't know how long after we got there.
But they told us that we all have to get on trucks.
And the way I know it was a Saturday is because the aunt, the mother of the little boy, she said, I don't go on trucks on Saturday.
Because I don't ride on Saturday.
And they said, well, you are getting on this truck.
And again, the beating started.
And her little boy, he screamed.
And he cried.
And he was yelling, you know, Mama, Mama.
[CRYING] And a lot of people were screaming and crying.
Because it was a place where they were separating people to go into different camps.
And [CRYING] they took my two aunts and us.
And they put us on these trucks, and some other people.
And they took us to a place called Ravensbruck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Ravensbruck, I think this other aunt that she lost her mind.
She lost her mind because she acted very, very strange.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And whatever they told her, she was saying, I'm not getting undressed in front of men, and give me back my sheitel.
[POINTS] And I don't want to eat non-kosher food.
And I want to go home.
And give me my child.
And they-- and my other aunt took the little boy.
And now she was in charge of the little boy because this, this aunt was, really already, she was-- she lost her mind.
And-- [CRYING] And my aunt, the one that was OK, she said to me, I want you to hold this aunt's hand.
And whenever they tell us to go someplace, you-- you just pull her.
Because I have to take care of the little boy.
And I have to take care of this other little girl, of her daughter.
And, and, and the third girl, she already knew how to walk by herself because she was close to my age.
So I, I kept holding the, the hand of this other aunt that [SOB] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was crazy already.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] I really hope whoever sees this tape, even a hundred years from today, will never forgive those Nazis for what they did to us.
[CRYING] So I was kind of pulling [SOBBING] my aunt, the other aunt, the one that was crazy.
And may she rest in peace.
And I was saying to her, don't cry and don't worry.
You're going to get back your sheitel.
And you're going to go back your clothes.
And you're going to get your little boy.
But you have to eat because if you don't eat, you're going to get hungry.
And you won't be able to take care of your little child.
And every time the Nazis came in, they would kick her, and beat her, and do all kinds of terrible things to her.
And we went-- one night, we went to sleep in our barracks.
And she got out of her [SOBS] bunk bed.
And she took her little boy.
And she started running to the door.
And she said, I'm going home.
And my aunt said to me, go run after her.
And [CRYING] I said, [CRYING] I said, you can't [SOBBING] go home because we don't have a home.
[CRYING] And she started banging on the door.
And then the Nazis came.
And my other aunt came and grabbed the child, the little boy, out of her arms.
And they took her away.
And we never saw her again.
Also.
No.
[CRYING] Only in Ravensbruck when we came, they gave us clothes with stripes.
They gave us these striped dresses.
And they gave us shoes, some kind of-- without laces-- but some kind of a shoe.
And they came with dogs, with a lot of dogs.
And these dogs jumped on a lot of us.
And a lot of people they bit, the dogs.
And they came with horses.
And they tried to run into us with the horses.
But the horses didn't want-- it's amazing.
The horses would not go on us.
They kept going ooh-ooh-ooh, you know?
And they kept standing up.
And then, they took us all to some big room.
And they shaved everybody's heads.
And they put numbers, not on our body, but on our dresses, they-- some kind of a number in, in a white-- there was something white on the dress.
And there was the number.
Do you remember how you lived in Ravensbruck?
Did you live in houses?
I remember it was barracks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and many, many people in the barracks, all of us infested with lice.
Because I remember that every night, we were scratching.
We were getting so bitten up.
And my aunt would say, you just have to try to bear it.
And she would go and get a rag with cold water.
And she would-- water, you know, whatever water there was.
And she would put it on us because we we're so bitten up.
And I remember everybody having terrible diarrhea and running to the bathrooms.
And all the time, there were these long lines.
The bathrooms were just like, you know, holes.
And we would stand on line.
And no sooner did you finish, you went back on the line because you could barely make it back again to the bathroom.
And I remember dying people, dying all at the time, not just from getting killed, but from dying from, from sickness, from starvation.
Because after a while, we couldn't make it to the bathrooms anymore.
So we just had to go outside.
And to get out of our barrack, to go to find a empty space, we had to go over these dead bodies.
And there were so many just bodies and bodies and bodies, dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I also remember that in the morning, every day, they would have us go through lines.
And at the head of the line-- there were two lines-- at the head of the line were two Nazis or Gestapos or whatever you call them.
And they would say to the right, to the left, to the right, to the left.
And I don't remember what was to the-- to life and what was to death.
But one of the lines, we never saw these people again.
And one of the other lines, you again got the chance tomorrow to go again through the same thing.
And I remember that I got a ear infection, terrible ear aches, terrible, terrible, terrible-- and pus just running down my, my head.

And we were sleeping on some kind of a mattresses that were filled with hay and my aunt taking the hay, and crushing it up, and sticking it in my ear, and saying you must not let that pus run down your ear.
Because if you do, you're going to go on the line that's for death.
And don't cry about it that it hurts you.
And if they ask you anything, don't say.
And I remember we were going through these lines.
And I was thinking, if this hurts, then how much more can death hurt?
So I'm not going to say anything.
And I remember a doctor in Ravensbruck.
I don't know whether he was assigned by the Germans to take care if somebody was sick, or whether he was just-- what he was.
Because there were no men there that I remember with us in out area.
And I remember that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many, many times, we would say to my aunt, I'm nauseous.
I'm so nauseous.
I'm so nauseous.
And my stomach hurts.
And I'm so sick.
And one time we met this man who said he's the doctor.
And he didn't give us anything.
But I don't know what it is that he-- how he helped us.
I just remember.
And I also remember-- but I don't remember where it was-- but I remember I was going into showers.
And this I can't remember, if it was in what camp.
But I know that people didn't want to go.
And the Nazis were pushing everybody.
And I remember my aunt saying to another woman, try not to go into the shower.
Because sometimes it's not a shower.
Sometimes it's gas that will come out.
And we won't come out there.
And we were pushed in there.
And-- but I don't remember where it was.
And it's very possible that I was in some other camp that I don't remember, that-- and I can't remember where.
But I just remember that I saw these showers, and that I was thinking, you know, are we going to come out of this?
Is just going to be water?
Is this going to be gas that is going to kill us?
And to this day, when I see a lot of metal in bathrooms, if it's in a hotel, I always tell my husband, I don't, I don't like these rooms.
I don't like to be here.
And I remember we were in Switzerland just recently.
And we went into a hotel.
And they had so many pipes.
And they were all this color of silver, you know, metal.
And I said, I don't want to go into the showers.
And my husband said, you have nothing to be afraid of.
This is such a long time ago.
And I said, I'm not-- I just don't want to stay in this hotel.
Yes there were a lot of children in Ravensbruck.
